And so that's not someplace where that person has been touched a lot. And the idea is that when you do that, and it will remind him of the last time he did it, even just like two minutes before. And by reminding him of it and reinforcing that, he's building that pattern in the person. And you can also do this over the phone by repeating a certain word like yes or excellent, or you could do this visually by increasing eye contact and nodding. Every time the person says something in that direction of being more loving or more present, you would give a little forceful extra eye contact and a nod. So there are different ways which are all anchoring the behavior. Yeah, I like those ways much better. It feels better to me than touching the person. And there's so many people that don't react well to being touched like that when they're in a relationship with a coach, when you're not supposed to be touched. And especially when you're on the phone, it's better to have a clue or word like for example, that's right, or that's it. And it serves the same purpose. So an anchor is any time where an emotion you can finally translate it into something very concrete, right? And therefore that thing will be a reminder of another time. I mean, with Tony, you may even discover an identity in someone or a nickname or a word that they like. That all can't – those are all – anchors are very broad things. Like, have you ever heard like a stand-up comic that begins with a joke and then they end and they're basically reminding you of the first joke they told you? That's an anchoring technique, meaning that he'd already anchored a certain laugh and a certain experience into that. And then he closes on it and it goes full circle and it feels like you kind of have like a complete experience from that. So anchoring is just a very broad technique. So touch is just one of them. So it's totally normal for any point in time, one child in the family to present with more emotions and more problems. But I would, in order to help him not have to do that so much, there's two different strategies I can think of. Chloe, Mark, let me know if you guys have others. One is a pretend strategy and the other is the life-ruiner strategy modified for a younger child. So you can actually come out in the open with the idea, with the life-ruiner strategy. And we use this in our own family when our children were between two and 12 years old, was to talk about how usually one child in the family acts out and they take it upon themselves to draw all the energy and attention to them so that the rest of the family does not have to worry about themselves or their relationships or other problems. But it's not fair for one child to always get to do that. So you would like to ask everyone to take turns and you could actually designate different days of the week where the... I don't know if you said six-year-old, but whatever age the different children are, they plan some way they're going to act out or be a little naughty. And that...because that day they're the one that gets to...that will draw the family's attention towards them. And so it's a pretend strategy. They're pretending to be naughty, but it brings an unconscious behavior out into the open and it actually takes...it elevates the, quote, problem child into the position of actually helping the family. They've been the one that has taken the negative energy of being punished or being disruptive so that everyone else could be peaceful and get along well. And it really is a very hard job to be the one having tantrum. It's amazing how it works. It really works. And then the other thing you could do just with that one child who has tantrums, decide at points in time when there's no negative behavior, no tantrums, that you say, hey, I want to pretend something with you. And you can say, I'm going to pretend to have a horrible tantrum and then I want you to help me calm down, tell me things to do. And you can start having insults, doing whatever that you could do sort of to mimic the tantrums he has and then allow him to help you calm down. And if he doesn't right away, you can say calming things to yourself like, I just have to remember that everyone loves me or I just have to remember that what I really need right now is food. I'm hungry. And so you model for him what it's like to lose control and regain control. And you allow him to help you lose control and regain control. And this will work for him to learn how to calm himself down and to give you suggestions for ways that when he's having the tantrum, instead of trying to control it, you're able to experience his energy, let it go, and know that it's a cycle and that his body wants to experience the calm again. So especially in children, you know, everyone has their own cycle but the emotions don't last that long.
